metadata.description: Comprehensive generation technology database aligned with the Open Energy Ontology (OEO). Designed for use with PyPSA, Calliope, OSeMOSYS, and ADOPTNet0 modeling frameworks. Covers small-scale PV (300 Wp balcony systems) through utility-scale solar, onshore wind (community single-turbine to 600 MW US Plains), fixed-bottom offshore wind (Baltic nearshore to 1.5 GW gigafarm), and floating offshore wind (Hywind Norway spar to 1 GW next-generation projections). Includes Germany-specific EEG, Bürgerwindpark, and North Sea EEZ contexts.
primary_sources: US EIA Capital Cost and Performance Characteristic Estimates 2020
HTW Berlin Yield Study – Steckersolare Systeme 2022

technologies.description: Large-scale ground-mounted photovoltaic power plants connected directly to transmission networks, typically >1 MW. Includes single-axis tracking systems.

technologies.description: Small to medium-scale rooftop or ground-mounted PV systems connected to distribution networks. Covers nano-residential (1–2 kW), residential (3–20 kW), small commercial (20–200 kW), commercial (200–500 kW), and industrial (0.5–5 MW) segments. Module technologies include PERC, TOPCon, and HJT.

technologies.description: Plug-in photovoltaic micro-generation units mounted on balconies, facades, terraces, or flat-roof parapets. Connected directly to the household circuit via a Schuko socket (EU) or equivalent. Typically 150–2000 W peak. Legally regulated in Germany under DIN VDE 0100-551-1 with a maximum feed-in of 800 W (2024 reform from 600 W). No grid operator notification required for systems ≤ 2 kW peak / ≤ 800 W inverter output in Germany.

technologies.description: Land-based wind turbines from single-turbine community installations (2–3 MW) to large utility farms (600+ MW). Covers six turbine classes: micro/community (2–3 MW, hub height 80–100 m), small commercial (3–5 MW), mid-size (5–7 MW, modern German standard), large modern (7–10 MW), next-generation (>10 MW). Capacity factors range from ~25% in low-wind interior sites to ~55% on US Great Plains. Includes German Bürgerwindpark, EEG auction, and repowering contexts.

technologies.description: Offshore wind turbines on fixed-bottom foundations (monopile, jacket, tripod, gravity-based) in water depths up to ~60 m. Turbine sizes range from 8 MW (2020s legacy) to 20+ MW (next-generation). Covers nearshore Baltic-type projects (<30 km, <30 m depth, jacket/monopile), standard North Sea farms (30–60 m, monopile), and gigafarm configurations. Specific turbine references include Siemens Gamesa SG 14-222 DD, Vestas V236-15, and GE Haliade-X 14 MW. Includes German EEZ, repowering, and multi-year cost projections.

instances.instance_name: Offshore Wind Fixed – 50 MW Nearshore Demo (Monopile, <20 m depth, Class 4)
instances.reference_source: IRENA Renewable Power Generation Costs 2023 – Small offshore; WindEurope Stats 2023

instances.reference_source: NREL ATB 2023 – Offshore Wind Fixed Class 3; WindEurope Baltic Stats 2023

technologies.description: Offshore wind turbines on floating sub-structures for water depths >60 m. Three platform types: spar-buoy (ballast stabilised, suitable for >120 m, e.g. Hywind Norway), semi-submersible (column-stabilised, deployable from 60 m, most commercial pipeline), and tension-leg platform (TLP, mooring-stabilised, lower motion, 60–200 m). Enables access to deep-water, high-wind resources unreachable by fixed-bottom foundations. CAPEX declines sharply post-2028 as supply chains, installation vessels, and port infrastructure mature. Capacity factors are typically higher than fixed-bottom due to superior wind resources at deep-water sites.

instances.reference_source: Equinor Hywind Norway / Hywind Tampen project data 2022; IEA Floating Offshore Wind Report 2022

technologies.description: Hydropower plants that use the natural flow of a river without significant water storage. Output depends on river discharge, providing relatively stable baseload with seasonal variation.

instances.instance_name: Run-of-River Hydro – 5 MW Small Plant (Alpine Stream)
instances.reference_source: IRENA Renewable Power Generation Costs 2023

instances.instance_name: Run-of-River Hydro – 50 MW Medium Plant
instances.reference_source: IEA Hydropower Special Report 2021

instances.instance_name: Run-of-River Hydro – 300 MW Large Plant (Major River)
instances.reference_source: World Bank Hydropower Cost Study 2022

technologies.description: Hydropower plants with a large upstream reservoir for water storage, enabling dispatchable generation independent of instantaneous river flow. Provides peaking, balancing, and long-duration storage services.

instances.instance_name: Reservoir Hydro – 200 MW Medium (Seasonal Storage)
instances.reference_source: IRENA Renewable Power Generation Costs 2023

instances.instance_name: Reservoir Hydro – 1000 MW Large Peaker
instances.reference_source: IEA Hydropower Special Report 2021

instances.instance_name: Reservoir Hydro – 3000 MW Major Dam (e.g., Hoover-class)
instances.reference_source: World Bank Hydropower Cost Study 2022

technologies.description: Combined-cycle power plants burning natural gas in a gas turbine and recovering exhaust heat in a heat recovery steam generator (HRSG) to drive a steam turbine. Highest thermal efficiency among gas-fired plants.

instances.instance_name: CCGT – 400 MW Standard Class (Current, 2024)
instances.reference_source: NREL ATB 2023 – Combined Cycle

instances.instance_name: CCGT – 800 MW Advanced H-Class (Current, 2024)
instances.reference_source: US EIA Capital Cost Estimates 2020

instances.instance_name: CCGT – 500 MW H2-Ready (Future, 2035)
instances.reference_source: IEA World Energy Outlook 2023 – Gas with CCS/H2

technologies.description: Simple-cycle combustion turbines burning natural gas, used primarily for peaking power and grid balancing due to their fast-start capability. Lower efficiency than CCGT but lower capital cost.

instances.instance_name: OCGT – 50 MW Aeroderivative Peaker (Current, 2024)
instances.reference_source: NREL ATB 2023 – Combustion Turbine

instances.instance_name: OCGT – 150 MW Frame Peaker (Current, 2024)

instances.instance_name: OCGT – 300 MW Large Frame Turbine (Current, 2024)
instances.reference_source: Lazard LCOE Analysis v16.0 (2023)

technologies.description: Reciprocating internal combustion engines (diesel or gas) used for distributed power generation, backup power, and island-grid applications. Fast-start and highly flexible. Can also run on biogas.

instances.instance_name: ICE – 5 MW Medium Gas Engine (Industrial/CHP)

instances.instance_name: ICE – 20 MW Large Engine Portfolio (Multi-unit Peaker)

technologies.description: Steam turbine power plants fuelled by coal. Includes pulverised coal combustion (PCC) at sub-critical, supercritical, and ultra-supercritical steam conditions. Higher steam parameters improve efficiency and reduce emissions.

instances.instance_name: Coal – 600 MW Supercritical PCC (New Build, 2024)

instances.reference_source: IEA Clean Coal Technologies Report 2021

technologies.description: Large-scale nuclear fission power plants producing baseload electricity. Includes Generation II (PWR, BWR) and Generation III/III+ reactors (AP1000, EPR). Very low operational CO2 emissions.

instances.instance_name: Nuclear – 1600 MW Gen III+ EPR (New Build, 2024)

instances.instance_name: Nuclear – 1100 MW Gen III AP1000 (New Build – US/Korea cost)

technologies.description: Nuclear reactors with electrical output typically below 300 MW. Factory-fabricated modules intended to reduce construction time and cost. Examples include NuScale, BWRX-300, and Rolls-Royce SMR.

technologies.description: Power generation using heat from the Earth's interior. Includes dry steam, flash steam, and binary cycle plants. Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS) are an emerging deep-resource technology.
